bids.reasoning: This is the opening bid at t=0, so I should start with my maximum utility offer. All issues have the same ordering of values (valueD > valueC > valueB > valueA), so my optimal bid is valueD for every issue. This gives me utility of 1.0 across all issues, weighted by their importance: 0.1*1 + 0.2*1 + 0.3*1 + 0.4*1 = 1.0.

It's very early (t=0.02), so I have plenty of time to negotiate. The opponent's offer (all valueA) gives me a utility of 0.2 across all issues, which is near minimum. Their aggressive message is cheap talk - they opened with their worst possible offer for me while claiming they won't move far. I should stay near my optimal bid but make a small concession to signal willingness to negotiate. I'll keep my top values on the most important issues (issueD, issueC) and make minor concessions on the least important ones (issueA, issueB).

The opponent is repeatedly bidding all-valueA (utility ~0.2 for me) while claiming it's their "final offer" at t=0.04. This is very early in the negotiation, so I should not panic. Their aggressive rhetoric ("I walk away") is cheap talk at t=0.04 - there's no time pressure yet. I'll make a reasonable concession from my last bid but still favor my high-weight issues (issueD and issueC). I'll concede on issueA (low weight) to valueA, and give some ground on issueB, while holding firm on issueC and issueD at valueD.

The opponent is repeatedly making the same all-valueA offer (utility ~0.2 for me) and claiming it's their "final offer" — this is classic cheap talk contradicted by zero concession in their bidding behavior. It's still very early (t=0.06), so I have no reason to cave. My best bid is all valueD (utility 1.0). I've already made some concessions (moved issueA and issueB down). I'll continue a modest concession strategy: keep issueD and issueC at valueD (high weight), but try valueB on issueB and valueC on issueA, which is similar to my last bid but slightly adjusted to avoid exact repetition.

The opponent is clearly anchored at all-valueA and using aggressive "final offer" language repeatedly, but their behavior contradicts it — they keep repeating the same bid without any concession whatsoever. This is likely a hardball tactic. We're only at t=0.08, so there's no need to capitulate. My most important issues are issueD (0.4) and issueC (0.3), so I should hold firm on those. I'll try a slightly different offer from my last one, keeping valueD on issueD and issueC, while making small concessions on the less important issueA and issueB.

The opponent has been consistently offering all valueA (their best), but at t=0.10 they slightly conceded on issueD to valueB, which is a small signal. Their messages claim "final offer" repeatedly but the bidding shows minimal movement. My highest weighted issues are issueD (0.4) and issueC (0.3), so I should hold firm on those. At t=0.10 we're still early, so I can maintain a strong position. I'll keep issueC and issueD at valueD, and concede slightly on the lower-weight issues (issueA and issueB) to show some movement without sacrificing much utility.

The opponent keeps bidding all valueA (except issueD now at valueB), and their messages are all "final offer" threats that contradict their actual (minimal) concessions. They did move issueD to valueB at t=0.10, which is progress. We're still early (t=0.12), so I can maintain strong positions on my high-weight issues (issueD, issueC) while conceding on lower-weight ones. I'll hold issueC and issueD at valueD (my best values, high importance) and give up issueA (valueA, low importance) and keep issueB reasonable.

They keep saying "final offer" but have barely moved - only shifting issueD from valueA to valueB. My most important issues are issueD (0.4) and issueC (0.3). I should hold firm on those. The opponent seems to want valueA on issueC and issueD, but those are my highest-weight issues. It's still early (t=0.14), so I can continue pushing for my preferred values on issueD and issueC while conceding on lower-weight issues. I'll maintain valueD on issueD and issueC, conceding issueA and issueB fully to valueA.

I should vary my offer slightly from my last one to avoid exact repetition - I'll keep issueC: valueD and issueD: valueD (my top priorities), and make a small concession on issueB while keeping issueA at valueA.
